blocks.statement: I can describe free fall as constant acceleration g, read g off a v–t slope, and calculate fall times for short drops.

blocks.whatWeKnow: 2026-XJ would fall at g if it were close enough to Earth.
blocks.connection: g is a NUMBER (9.81 m/s² near Earth). Today you read it off a graph and use it. Same g for a coin, a feather, and an asteroid.

blocks.markdown:
## Everything falls at g

Drop anything near Earth's surface (and ignore air): its velocity grows by **9.81 m/s every second**. That steady growth is an acceleration, and it has a name: **g**.

Two equations do all of today's work:

- **v = g·t** — how fast after falling for time t
- **y = ½·g·t²** — how far it has fallen after time t

On a **velocity–time graph** of a falling object, the line is straight and its **slope is g**. That's not a coincidence — it's the definition of acceleration from Unit 1, with gravity setting the rate.

terms.definition: Motion under gravity only — no air resistance, no other forces. A ball dropped 1 m takes about 0.45 s to fall (ignoring air). All objects fall the same.
terms.cognate: Sp. caída libre · Pt. queda livre · HC chit lib

blocks.title: Free fall doesn't mean 'floating'
blocks.markdown: Free fall means gravity is the **only** force acting. Falling IS the motion — a skydiver before the parachute, a dropped coin, and an orbiting station are all in free fall.

blocks.title: v–t graph for a dropped object
Read the graph like a physicist
blocks.markdown: Pick any two points on the line. Δv between them, divided by Δt, gives the slope. You should get **≈ 9.8 m/s²** no matter which two points you pick — that's what 'constant acceleration' looks like. Try the cliff-drop simulation below and watch the v–t graph build.

blocks.prompt: A coin is dropped from rest off a 10 m balcony. How long until it hits the ground? (Ignore air. Use y = ½·g·t².)
blocks.answer: ≈ 1.43 s. The coin falls for about a second and a half.

blocks.prompt: A pebble is dropped from a 100 m bridge. How long until it hits the water? Show your work.
blocks.givenHint: The fall height, the starting velocity (it's dropped), and g.
blocks.equationHint: The fall-distance equation: y = ½·g·t². Solve it for t.

blocks.prompt: How long does it take a coin to fall 1 m from rest, ignoring air? Show your work — then complete the frame about the v–t graph.
blocks.frame: t ≈ ___ s. On the v–t graph for a falling object, the slope is ___, because v increases by ___ every second.
